title: Performance analysis of a hybrid photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) collector with integrated CPC troughs
abstract:
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ENERGY RESEARCH
Int. J. Energy Res. 23: 1295}1304 (1999)
PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS OF A HYBRID
PHOTOVOLTAIC/THERMAL (PV/T) COLLECTOR WITH
INTEGRATED CPC TROUGHS
H. P. GARG* AND R. S. ADHIKARI
Centre for Energy Studies, Indian Institute of Technology, Hauz Khas, New Delhi-110 016, India
SUMMARY
In the present investigation a theoretical analysis has been presented for the modelling of thermal and electrical processes
of a hybrid PV/T air heating collector coupled with a compound parabolic concentrator (CPC). In this design, several
CPC troughs are combined in a single PV/T collector panel. The absorber of the hybrid PV/T collector under
investigation consists of an array of solar cells for generation of electricity, while collector #uid circulating past the
absorber provides useful thermal energy as in a conventional #at plate collector. In the analysis, it is assumed that solar
cell e$ciency can be represented by a linear decreasing function of its temperature. Energy balance equations have been
developed for the various components of the system. Based on the developed analysis, both thermal and electrical
performance of the system as a function of system design parameters are presented and discussed. Results have been
presented to compare the performance of hybrid PV/T collector coupled with and without CPC. Copyright ( 1999
John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEY WORDS:
hybrid photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T); air heating collector; compound parabolic concentrator (CPC)
1. INTRODUCTION
Solar energy is a valuable form of energy which has the potential to meet a signi"cant proportion of the
world's energy needs. The major applications of solar energy include solar collectors and solar photovoltaic
systems. Solar collectors are designed to generate thermal energy; however, photovoltaic cell produces
electricity directly from solar energy. During photovoltaic energy conversion, thermal energy is also
generated which results in increased cell temperature. It is well-known fact that the e$ciency of PV cells
drops as the temperature of cell increases. As a result, this heat generation within a photovoltaic module has
traditionally been viewed as a hindrance to e$ciency and subsequently e!orts have been made to transfer the
thermal energy of PV module to the surrounding air. It has been shown that this heat can be harnessed by
combining both the thermal and photovoltaic system in a hybrid photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) energy
system. The normal #at plate collector is converted into a hybrid system by pasting solar cells directly over
the absorber plate.
In the literature, a number of theoretical as well as experimental studies (Floreschetez, 1979; Hendrie, 1980;
Hendrie and Raghuraman, 1980; Cox and Raghuraman, 1985; Chandra et al., 1983; Agarwal, 1989) have
been made on the hybrid PV/T systems. Garg and his collaborators (Bhargava et al., 1991; Garg et al., 1991,
1994; Agarwal and Garg, 1994; Garg and Agarwal, 1995) have carried out detailed analytical and experi-
mental studies on hybrid PV/T air and liquid heating systems. Sopian et al. (1995) have presented a
steady-state model for the performance prediction of single and double pass PV/T air collector. A research
* Correspondence to: H. P. Garg, Centre for Energy Studies, Indian Institute of Technology, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India.
Email: garghp@ces.iitd.ernet.in
CCC 0363}907X/99/151295}10$17.50
Received 5 January 1999
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Accepted 27 April 1999
 project (1995) to explore and identify the PV hybrid concept has been carried out by IT Power and New
Castle Photovoltaic Application Centre, commissioned by the Joint Research Centre at ISPRA. Recently,
Bergene and Lovvik (1995) have proposed a detailed physical model of hybrid PV/T thermal system using
water as a heat transfer #uid for the prediction of system performance.
Studies on hybrid PV/T systems are being made at Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) in cooperation
with All India Council of Technical Education (AICTE), New Delhi, India. Earlier studies made by the
present authors (Garg and Adhikari, 1997, 1998a) includes the development of steady state and transient
simulation models to predict the performance of hybrid PV/T air heating collectors. These studies have
shown that hybrid PV/T systems have great potential in terms of their system e$ciencies. It has been also
observed that in a hybrid PV/T air heating system, a large area of solar cells is required; therefore, one must
look for some avenues for enhancing the electrical output of the system keeping the cell area at a low level. It
has been envisaged that a promising cost-reduction technique could be its e!ective coupling with a solar
concentrating device. As a result, the output of solar cell would be increased as more solar insolation falls
on the cell. Moreover, for a given electrical output, the system cost would be less, as optical concentrating
devices are cheaper than the solar cells.
In the past, several novel designs of optical concentrating device for solar energy collection have been
developed in order to reach high collecting temperatures with high e$ciency. The compound parabolic
concentrator (CPC) is the most promising high-concentration device which produces elevated operating
temperature and represents the optimum optical properties (Winston, 1974; Rabl, 1976). Recently, present
authors (Garg and Adhikari, 1998b) have made optical design calculations of a CPC suitable for its coupling
with a hybrid PV/T system. Results have been presented for a full and truncated 3X CPC.
In the present investigation a theoretical analysis has been presented for the modelling of thermal and
electrical processes of a hybrid PV/T collector coupled with CPC. The system under investigation consists of
several CPC troughs combined in a single PV/T collector panel. Based on detailed heat transfer analysis the
energy balance equations have been developed for each component of the system. Thermal and electrical
performance of the system as a function of system design parameters are presented and discussed.
2. THEORETICAL ANALYSIS
The system under investigation along with associated thermal energy network have been shown in Figure 1.
The system consists of several troughs combined in a single PV/T collector panel, a metallic black absorber

In the present investigation a theoretical analysis has been presented for the modelling of thermal and
electrical processes of a hybrid PV/T collector coupled with CPC. The system under investigation consists of
several CPC troughs combined in a single PV/T collector panel. Based on detailed heat transfer analysis the
energy balance equations have been developed for each component of the system. Thermal and electrical
performance of the system as a function of system design parameters are presented and discussed.
The system under investigation along with associated thermal energy network have been shown in Figure 1.
The system consists of several troughs combined in a single PV/T collector panel, a metallic black absorber
Figure 1. Hybrid PV/T collector coupled with CPC along with associated thermal energy network
Int. J. Energy Res. 23: 1295}1304 (1999)
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
plate and a well-insulated rear plate. In this con"guration inlet air is passed through the passage between
absorber and rear plate. The absorber plate is either a black paint or selective surface and solar cells are
pasted over it. The adhesive used for pasting solar cells is characterized by high thermal conductive and good
electric insulating material. Photovoltaic cells are pasted over 1 m wide absorber plate at equal distances in
equal number of rows and columns per unit area of absorber surface.
In order to simplify the theoretical analysis, the following assumptions have been made:
1. Steady state of energy transfer has been achieved.
2. The CPC is ideal and free from fabrication errors and any beam of radiation incident within the
acceptance angle, with the help of parabolic re#ector, can reach the receiver.
3. The variation of nN (average number of re#ections for radiation passing through CPC inside the
acceptance angle) with the incidence angle is small. Hence nN is treated as constant during the whole
4. The temperatures of di!erent system components viz. transparent cover, solar cell, absorber plate,
rear plate vary along the direction of working #uid #ow only.
5. Heat capacity of transparent cover is very small and, therefore, neglected.
6. There is no leakage of air from the system.
7. Side losses from the system are negligible.

developed by Rabl (1976). The convective and radiative heat transfer coe$cients from transparent cover to
ambient h'~!; the conductive and radiative heat transfer coe$cients h1~", h"~! were calculated using the
relations given in Du$e and Beckman (1991). The forced convective heat transfer coe$cients h1~& and

For the quantitative appreciation of developed model calculations have been made for PV/T air heating
systems coupled with and without CPC. An algorithm has been developed to predict the working #uid (air)
temperature as a function of absorber length. System performance has been discussed corresponding to
di!erent design and operational parameters. Various thermo-physical parameter and weather data used in
the calculation have been summarised in Table 1. The optical design parameters of a truncated 3X CPC have
been taken from Garg and Adhikari (1998b). The results have been presented and discussed in the subsequent
Figures 2 and 3 show the thermal e$ciency curves for PV/T collector with and without CPC con"gura-
tions for a "xed duct depth, collector length, mass #ow rate and packing fraction (area covered by solar cells)
corresponding to the absorber with and without selective coating. It has been observed that for both
non-selective and selective absorbers, initially the performance of the system without CPC is better; however,
after a certain point the system with CPC performs better. It implies that the integration of a CPC with PV/T
system is appropriate for the application in the higher temperature range. For both the con"gurations, with
and without CPC, the system performs better in the case of selective absorber. This is due to reduction in
radiative heat losses from absorber to glass cover.
The results of the parametric studies have been presented in subsequent "gures. Figure 4 presents the
variation of thermal and electric output as a function of duct depth for a "xed collector length, packing
fraction (cell density) and air mass #ow rate. It has been observed that both the thermal and electric output
decrease with an increase in duct depth for con"gurations with and without CPC. However, the percentage
decrease in system output for larger values of duct depth is small. This characteristic may be attributed to the
decreasing absorber to air heat transfer coe$cient as the duct depth increases. Lower electric output for
larger duct depth is a consequence of the relatively higher temperature of the absorber plate-cum-solar cell.
HYBRID PHOTOVOLTAIC/THERMAL COLLECTOR

Figure 4. Variation of system performance output as a function of duct depth
Figure 5. Variation of system performance output as a function of collector length
The results of variation of thermal and electric output as a function of collector length have been shown in
Figure 5. It is seen that the system performance increases with an increase in collector length; however, the
percentage increase in performance output decrease for the larger values of collector length. This suggests
that an optimum value of collector length can be obtained for "xed values of system and design parameters.
Figure 6 shows the variation of thermal and electrical output as a function of air-mass #ow rate
corresponding to the system con"gurations with and without CPC. It has been seen that increasing mass
#ow rate increases the thermal and electrical output for both the con"gurations. Higher mass #ow rate
results in a lower temperature of the absorber plate. As a consequence, the cell e$ciency is higher for high air
mass #ow rate which results in higher electric output. It is also quite evident that the performance output of
the system with CPC is quite higher than without CPC.
The e!ect of packing fraction of solar cells on the performance of the system has been shown in Figure 7.
This indicates that increasing the area covered by solar cells increases electrical output of the system quite
HYBRID PHOTOVOLTAIC/THERMAL COLLECTOR

Int. J. Energy Res. 23: 1295}1304 (1999)
Figure 6. Variation of system performance output as a function of air-mass #ow rate
Figure 7. E!ect of packing fraction (area covered by solar cells) on performance output
rapidly. However, the thermal output remains more or less same. The system coupled with CPC shows better
performance in terms of both the thermal and electrical output.
The developed steady-state model predicts the thermal and electric performance of a hybrid PV/T air heating
collector coupled with a CPC. Results show that the coupling of CPC with a PV/T air heating collector
depends on the temperature range for which the system is designed. Parametric studies show that the thermal
and electric output of a PV/T system increases with increase in collector length, air mass #ow rate and
Int. J. Energy Res. 23: 1295}1304 (1999)
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
packing fraction (cell density), and decreases with increase in duct depth. It has also been observed that the
system coupled with CPC always performs better in terms of both the thermal and electric output. It is to
be noted that as the material cost increases with the integration of more number of cells and the CPC in the
system, the "nal decision in this respect must be based on the cost-e!ectiveness of the system by minimising
life-cycle cost (LCC) of the system.
